content.text: Claude Code's headless mode lets you run Claude non-interactively — in scripts, CI pipelines, editor extensions, and programmatic tools — without spawning a terminal session. Two surfaces exist: the
. Both share the same underlying agent loop — you get full tool access, session state, and MCP connectivity, just without a human at the keyboard.

attrs.alt: Headless mode usage patterns — CLI print mode, stdin pipe, TypeScript SDK, and CI automation all connect to the Claude Code Engine
attrs.title: Claude Code headless integration patterns

content.text: ) to run a single prompt and exit. Claude Code runs the full agent loop, may call tools, and then prints the final assistant text to stdout.

# Print final answer to stdout
claude -p "What files changed in the last commit?"

# Capture result in a shell variable
SUMMARY=$(claude -p "Summarize this file" --file README.md)
echo "$SUMMARY"

# Pipe content in
cat error.log | claude -p "What is causing this error?"

# Chain multiple calls
FUNCS=$(claude -p "List all exported functions" < src/api.ts)
claude -p "Write JSDoc for these: $FUNCS"
on error. You can safely use
flag controls what gets written to stdout:
Full result object (content, cost, session_id, etc.)
Programs that need metadata or costs
Newline-delimited JSON events as they arrive
Streaming UIs, progress indicators, log ingestion

# JSON output — parse with jq
claude -p "Count lines in main.ts" --output-format json | jq '.result'

# Stream-JSON — handle events line by line
claude -p "Refactor auth.ts" --output-format stream-json | while IFS= read -r line; do
  TYPE=$(echo "$line" | jq -r '.type')
  if [ "$TYPE" = "assistant" ]; then
    echo "$line" | jq -r '.message.content[0].text // empty'
  fi
done
A session is a named conversation history. Headless mode can reuse sessions to build multi-turn workflows without repeating context.

# First call — capture the session ID
RESULT=$(claude -p "Read package.json and list all dependencies" --output-format json)
SESSION_ID=$(echo "$RESULT" | jq -r '.session_id')

# Follow-up call — resume the same conversation
claude -p "Which of those deps are outdated?" --resume "$SESSION_ID"

# --continue resumes the most recent session automatically
claude -p "Now check for security advisories" --continue
— reload a specific session by its UUID. The full conversation history (tool calls, results, prior messages) is restored before the new prompt is added.
— resume whichever session was used most recently on this machine. Convenient for interactive follow-ups from shell scripts.
By default the agent loop runs until Claude declares the task complete, up to a safety maximum. Two flags let you bound this:

# Stop after at most 5 turns (each tool call + response is one turn)
claude -p "Audit the test suite" --max-turns 5

# Combine with --output-format json to see how many turns ran
claude -p "Fix the failing test" --max-turns 10 --output-format json | jq '{turns: .num_turns, result: .result}'
when you want to cap API cost or prevent runaway loops in automated contexts. If the limit is hit before the task finishes, Claude returns whatever partial result it has.
npm package wraps the CLI in a typed programmatic interface. It spawns a managed Claude Code subprocess, communicates over
, and exposes each event as an async iterable.

In interactive mode, Claude Code prompts the user before running potentially destructive tools. In headless mode there is no user to ask, so you must declare permissions in advance.

# Allow only read-only tools — safe for analysis tasks
claude -p "Find all TODO comments" \
  --allowedTools "Read,Glob,Grep" \
  --output-format text

# Allow everything — use with care in CI
claude -p "Fix the broken build" \
  --dangerously-skip-permissions \
  --max-turns 20

# Restrict by tool category — block file writes
claude -p "Analyze the test coverage" \
  --disallowedTools "Write,Edit,Bash"

lets Claude run any tool, including Bash commands that delete files or make network requests. Only use this when the environment is sandboxed (a Docker container, a CI runner that discards state after the job) or when you trust the prompt source completely.
Headless mode is designed for automation. The most common CI pattern is: install Claude Code, export your Anthropic API key, run

# .github/workflows/ai-review.yml
name: AI Code Review
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  review:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      pull-requests: write
      contents: read

    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 0

      - name: Install Claude Code
        run: npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code

      - name: Run AI review
        env:
          ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.ANTHROPIC_API_KEY }}
        run: |
          DIFF=$(git diff origin/main...HEAD)
          REVIEW=$(claude -p "Review this diff for bugs, security issues, and style problems:\n$DIFF" \
            --output-format json \
            --allowedTools "" \
            --max-turns 3)

          # Check for error
          if [ "$(echo $REVIEW | jq -r '.is_error')" = "true" ]; then
            echo "Claude returned an error"
            exit 1
          fi

          echo "$REVIEW" | jq -r '.result' > review.txt
          cat review.txt

      - name: Post review comment
        uses: actions/github-script@v7
        with:
          script: |
            const fs = require('fs');
            const review = fs.readFileSync('review.txt', 'utf8');
            github.rest.issues.createComment({
              issue_number: context.issue.number,
              owner: context.repo.owner,
              repo: context.repo.repo,
              body: '## AI Code Review\n\n' + review
            });
For tasks too large for a single prompt, use session resumption across multiple CLI invocations:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

# Step 1: Explore the codebase
RESULT1=$(claude -p "List all database models and their relationships" \
  --output-format json --allowedTools "Read,Glob,Grep")
SESSION=$(echo "$RESULT1" | jq -r '.session_id')
echo "Phase 1 complete, session: $SESSION"

# Step 2: Build on what Claude learned
RESULT2=$(claude -p "Now identify which models are missing indexes" \
  --resume "$SESSION" \
  --output-format json --allowedTools "Read,Glob,Grep")
echo "$RESULT2" | jq -r '.result'

# Step 3: Generate migrations (allow file writes)
claude -p "Write migration files to add the missing indexes" \
  --resume "$SESSION" \
  --allowedTools "Read,Glob,Grep,Write" \
  --max-turns 10

Point to a proxy or custom endpoint.

Claude Code checks if stdout is a terminal. When piping to another process or
explicitly — without it, the interactive UI may try to render and corrupt your JSON.
Tool call logs, spinners, and debug output go to stderr; the final result or events go to stdout. Redirect stderr to
or a log file when parsing stdout programmatically.

If you embed untrusted input (PR descriptions, issue bodies, file content from forks) directly into the prompt, an attacker could craft content that hijacks Claude's instructions. Sanitize or fence untrusted content inside delimiters like
and tell Claude in the system prompt to treat it as data, not instructions.
Sessions are stored locally by the Claude Code binary. A session ID from one machine cannot be resumed on another unless you also transfer the session store (default:
Complex coding tasks can easily require 10–20 turns (read file → understand → edit → verify). Setting
on an open-ended task causes Claude to return a partial result without error.
Headless vs Interactive: When to Use Each
CI/CD pipeline step (lint, review, release notes)
Editor extension integrating Claude into a UI
Batch processing many files or repos

in unattended environments; never skip this for automated code-modifying tasks.
Sanitize untrusted input in CI contexts to prevent prompt injection attacks.
